It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Transparency in multilateral climate politics: Furthering (or distracting from) accountability? Aarti Gupta * Environmental Policy Group, Department of Social Sciences, Wageningen University, Wageningen, The Netherlands Harro van Asselt University of Eastern Finland, Law School, Joensuu, Finland Stockholm Environment Institute, Oxford, UK Abstract This article analyzes the interplay between transparency and accountability in multilateral climate politics. The 2015 Paris Agreement calls for a “pledge-and-review” approach to collective climate action with an “enhanced transparency framework” as a key pillar of the Agreement. By making visible who is doing what, transparency is widely assumed to be vital to holding countries to account and building trust. We explore whether transparency is generating such effects in this context, by developing and applying an analytical framework to examine the link between transparency and accountability. We ﬁnd that the scope and practices of climate transparency reﬂect (rather than necessarily reduce) broader conﬂicts over who should be held to account to whom and about what, with regard to responsibility and burden sharing for ambitious climate action. We conclude that the relationship between transparency and accountability is less straightforward than assumed, and that the transformative promise of transparency needs to be reconsidered in this light. Keywords: accountability, climate governance, Paris Agreement, transparency, United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). 1. Introduction This article analyzes the relationship between transparency and accountability in the multilateral climate regime established under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). In a widely acclaimed development, a new agreement was adopted in Paris in December 2015 to apply to the post-2020 phase of collective action to address climate change. The Paris Agreement, which entered into force in November 2016, embodies a “pledge-and-review” approach to climate action, with individual country input to a shared global effort taking the form of “nationally determined contributions” (NDCs). Under this approach, transparency of action and (ﬁnancial, technological, and capacity-building) support now takes center stage with an “enhanced transparency framework” made a key pillar of the Agreement. Much hope is being pinned on a multilaterally agreed transparency system to generate and share information about countries’ actions. Transparency is assumed to be vital to making visible who is doing what in this context, thus facilitating holding countries to account for their action or inaction. Through such holding to account, it is also assumed that transparency will enhance countries’ trust in each other, thereby also improving the prospects for ambitious global climate action. Is the climate regime’s transparency system realizing such high hopes? In particular, does transparency further accountability in this context? This is a timely and urgent question with high scholarly and policy currency, yet it remains little analyzed. Given the continual evoking of the need for transparency in contested arenas of global governance such as climate change, it is striking that the relationship between transparency and accountability remains more assumed than scrutinized. This application of our framework to the climate realm is intended to be exploratory, rather than a comprehensive empirical assessment of UNFCCC transparency arrangements to date. Because the details of the enhanced transparency framework under the Paris Agreement are still subject to negotiation, we focus largely on existing UNFCCC transparency arrangements in our analysis. Given that these arrangements are themselves relatively recent, little analysis has been conducted on how they are functioning, particularly in the academic literature. Thus, it is timely to undertake such an exploratory analysis, given that the transparency framework of the Paris Agreement will build on existing arrangements. Our empirical analysis is based on primary and secondary literature. The most important primary sources we draw on are UNFCCC decisions taken by parties on the scope and design of transparency arrangements, as well as country submissions on transparency-related issues to the UNFCCC prior to speciﬁc meetings. We also draw on intermittent observation of UNFCCC negotiating sessions on the transparency framework over the period 2013–2016.1 The article proceeds as follows: Section 2 reviews diverse conceptualizations of accountability and transparency (and their relationship) within existing international relations and global environmental governance literature. Drawing on this review, it advances an analytical framework through which to assess the transparency-accountability relationship. Section 3 provides an overview of the transparency arrangements developed within the UNFCCC, including those in the Paris Agreement. Section 4 then applies our analytical framework to assessing the relationship between transparency and accountability in multilateral climate politics. In Section 5, we present our conclusions. Our analysis suggests that rather than standing apart from or helping to mitigate accountability conﬂicts, transparency arrangements mirror such conﬂicts in their scope and design, with implications for the transformative potential of transparency. We conclude by drawing out these implications further and outlining elements of a future research agenda on the accountability–transparency relationship. 2. Accountability and transparency: Conceptualizing the links Individually, the concepts of transparency and accountability have been the subject of increasing scholarly attention in recent years. While transparency has recently received more attention in global environmental governance (Langley 2001; Hale 2008; Gupta 2008, 2010; Gupta & Mason 2014), accountability has a signiﬁcantly longer pedigree in this and related ﬁelds (e.g. Keohane 2006; Bovens 2007; Ebrahim & Weisband 2007; Newell 2008; Biermann & Gupta 2011). With regard to examining the relationship between the two, however, we remain largely in the realm of unexamined assertions (but see Fox 2007). The two terms are sometimes used interchangeably or transparency is simply assumed to be an essential prerequisite for greater accountability. How this relationship works in practice, and whether greater transparency does indeed further accountability has not yet been systematically analyzed. In assessing this relationship, we can begin from two distinct vantage points: that of transparency or that of accountability. From the vantage point of transparency, one relevant starting question is whether, and under what conditions, speciﬁc instances of transparency actually aim to further accountability, or whether distinct (and potentially competing) rationales are driving the uptake of transparency in global governance instead. In an era of neoliberal environmental governance, that is, governance characterized by a move toward market- based solutions, privatization, and reliance on voluntary approaches (Bernstein 2001), diverse rationales beyond furthering democratic accountability may well drive the growing embrace of transparency. These include, for example: a marketization rationale, whereby disclosure of information is essential to facilitating the creation and/or functioning of markets in environmental services; a privatization rationale, whereby disclosure facilitates the investment decisions of private actors as a means to bolster private authority; or a technocratic rationale, whereby disclosure of “more and better information” is intended to improve and rationalize (bureaucratic) decisionmaking. From a transparency vantage point, one can then analyze whether the uptake of disclosure is even driven by a rationale to enhance democratic accountability, and, if so, whether such an accountability rationale is being superseded in practice by alternative rationales for disclosure (Gupta & Mason 2016). We could also analyze the relationship between transparency and accountability from the vantage point of accountability. Here, the point of departure would be how accountability is being conceptualized and A. Gupta and H. van Asselt Transparency in climate politics 2 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 3: operationalized in a given issue area. Such an analysis can then illuminate what the prospects might be for transparency to serve as a mechanism by which to further accountability, once the contours of a given accountability relationship are clearer. This latter vantage point is the one we adopt in this article. Our focus, furthermore, is on accountability in multilateral state-led governance, that is, state-to-state accountability, but in a manner that goes beyond a principal– agent or delegated accountability model assumed to prevail in (domestic) public accountability settings. In multilateral state-to-state accountability contexts, the same actor is both principal and agent (given that states demand accountability from, and are accountable to, each other). We consider a focus on state-to-state accountability to be important, given an evolving global environmental governance research context that increasingly prioritizes the study of private and hybrid governance (e.g. Bäckstrand 2008; Chan & Pattberg 2008) and the accountability challenges herein. Much effort has been expended within recent literature on developing typologies of “plural accountabilities” (Bäckstrand 2006) discernible in multi-actor, hybrid, and networked modes of environmental governance. While this has yielded many useful insights, we see two risks here. First, such a focus may distract attention from the continuing need to scrutinize state-to-state accountability, particularly in contested issue areas such as climate change. Second, there is a risk of proliferating typologies. Our aim here is thus to draw on existing insights in the accountability literature to develop an analytical framework that is broadly applicable across diverse governance contexts. Furthermore, we are interested in integrating a systematic assessment of the transparency-accountability relationship into such an analytical framework. We turn next to outlining this framework, which we develop by ﬁrst disaggregating the component elements of accountability, and then outlining the associated transparency dimensions for each. 2.1. Conceptualizing accountability: Disaggregating its component elements In exploring the relationship between transparency and accountability, we take here as our point of departure two long- acknowledged aspects of accountability relations: answerability and enforceability. As articulated by Newell, drawing on Schedler et al. (1999), answerability implies “both the right to … demand a justiﬁcation for (in-)action on the part of an institution exercising power and the expectation on the part of an accountability ‘provider’ that it has to provide an account of its (in-)actions in the public name” (Newell 2008, p. 124; italics added). Enforceability refers to “the means to secure accountability: the ability to realize accountability claims and the capacity to penalize non-responsive behaviour with sanctions of one form or another” (Newell 2008, p. 124). While this parsing out of accountability into answerability and enforceability is a useful point of departure, it is necessary, in our view, to further disaggregate each in order to assess the role that transparency might play in furthering accountability. We thus identify ﬁve disaggregated elements that together, we argue, constitute the “answerability” and “enforceability” aspects of accountability. These include: relations, standards, judgments, sanctions, and redress (see also Biermann & Gupta 2011). We understand each of these to mean the following: • Relations: agreeing on who is to be held to account (and to whom); • Standards: agreeing on standards of performance against which to be held to account (i.e. agreeing on accountability for what); • Judgments: agreeing on a process by which to assess if standards are being met (i.e. the how of accountability); • Sanctions: agreeing on (legal, reputational, ﬁnancial) penalties if standards are not met; and • Redress: agreeing on the scope and modalities of liability and compensation for harm inﬂicted as a result of standards not being met. The ﬁrst three elements constitute the answerability component of accountability, with the latter two constituting the enforceability component. Securing answerability necessarily requires agreement on who is to be held to account and by whom, according to what standards of performance and by what process of judging whether the performance standards are being met. Enforceability likewise requires the ability to sanction non-compliant behavior if standards are not met, and the prospect of holding liable and securing compensation for harm done as a result of standards not being met. If these are the disaggregated elements of an accountability relationship, how might transparency serve to further accountability? We turn next to this aspect. Transparency in climate politics A. Furthering accountability through transparency We utilize the above disaggregated conception of accountability to identify corresponding transparency arrangements in a multilateral or transnational governance context, which would be required for transparency to be able to further accountability. We do so by categorizing transparency arrangements according to the same disaggregated accountability elements, and identifying key questions to be asked about such arrangements, relating back to the “who, about what, and how” of accountability. This yields the analytical framework in Figure 1. This analytical framework strengthens existing analyzes of accountability in several important ways. First, in disaggregating the component elements of accountability, the framework brings together under one umbrella, and makes explicit, the core elements of accountability and their inter-relationships to each other. Second, the framework highlights that these core set of accountability components hold across a diverse array of governance contexts, whether global, multilateral, transnational, hybrid, or networked settings, insofar as the accountability questions associated with each are the same across all such settings, even if the answers may vary. Thus, the framework can be used to identify and analyze the nature, scope, design, and degree of institutionalization of these core accountability elements in diverse governance settings. Third, the framework allows us to unpack the ex-ante and ex-post dimensions of accountability, an aspect that is particularly timely and is less elaborated in existing analyses. Ex-post accountability has long been associated with multilateral environmental agreements in the form of reporting and review related to the implementation of obligations (Raustiala 2001), as well as in seeking to hold to account for harm done (often characterized as a “liability model of accountability;” see Mason 2008). There is, nonetheless, a growing need for, and a speciﬁc set of challenges associated with, ex-ante accountability in contested areas of anticipatory governance, such as climate change (Biermann & Gupta 2011). Ex-ante accountability in the climate context comes into play when assessing (and holding countries to account) not only for existing obligations but also for (the ambition and fairness of) their intended actions in the future. Yet such an enterprise is fraught with fundamental scientiﬁc and political uncertainties. Ex-ante accountability is thus likely to be a site of conﬂict and negotiation in multilateral climate politics in the years ahead, but has received relatively little sustained scholarly attention. Finally, as its main intended contribution here, the analytical framework permits a more systematic assessment of how, and under what conditions, transparency arrangements might further accountability. The disaggregation into ﬁve component elements of accountability helps to discern a set of associated transparency questions (listed in Figure 1) to be scrutinized in any given instance to ascertain how transparency might help further accountability. In doing so, our analytical framework also makes clear that the role of transparency in accountability extends beyond what we refer to as the judgment stage, that is, beyond a scrutiny of the reporting and review system as Figure 1 Furthering accountability through transparency: Analytical framework. A. Gupta and H. van Asselt Transparency in climate politics 4 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 5: constituting the sum total of this relationship. Such reporting and review systems do constitute a critical core of transparency, as they specify the institutional arrangements and processes through which information is generated, shared, and reviewed, and hence, country actions are made visible. As we see it, however, it is equally critical to engage with the prior component elements of accountability, namely the relations and standards of performance (the “who and what”), and how they shape transparency. In highly contested global environmental governance challenges such as climate change, ﬁrst-order questions pertaining to “who is to be accountable to whom for what” fundamentally shape the design of review (i.e. the how of transparency arrangements). Thus, our point of departure here is that focusing only on the judgment phase risks missing out on the broader context that shapes the transparency–accountability relationship and the prospects for transparency to do its transformative work. In taking a more holistic view, this analytical framework allows us to comprehensively explore whether and how the scope and practices of transparency are shaped by, reﬂect, or help mitigate the contested politics of accountability in global environmental governance. We turn next to assessing the transparency–accountability relationship in multilateral climate politics, with one important caveat. While our analytical framework above includes, for the sake of completeness, both the answerability and enforceability components of the accountability–transparency relationship, we focus in the empirical section of this article on the answerability dimension of this relationship. This is because the enforcement component (even sanctions, much less liability for not meeting agreed-upon standards of performance) is largely absent or only very vaguely delineated in UNFCCC provisions, also under the Paris Agreement (except for the Kyoto Protocol; see Section 4.2). There is correspondingly little to no envisaged role for transparency in the enforceability component of accountability relations in this speciﬁc multilateral context. As a result, we focus on the three disaggregated elements of the answerability dimension of accountability (relations, standards, and judgments) and the link to transparency herein. Where relevant, we touch upon the role of transparency arrangements in reaching a judgment about compliance with agreed-upon standards of performance (as the culmination of reporting and review processes, i.e, the judgment phase). Such a judgment then feeds into the UNFCCC’s largely facilitative compliance arrangements, where they exist, as a distinct and prior step to enforcement, insofar as these arrangements focus on facilitating the process of meeting agreed-upon performance standards, rather than on sanctioning or ascribing liability and redress for lack of adherence to agreed-upon standards of performance. Thus, our analysis covers answerability (which can culminate in a judgment of compliance or non-compliance) but not enforcement, understood as sanctions and/or liability for lack of compliance. 3. Transparency arrangements in the multilateral climate regime: An overview Based on the obligations contained in the UNFCCC and the Kyoto Protocol, elaborate transparency arrangements have been developed under the climate regime over the past two decades. As regimes are rarely constructed on a blank slate, the transparency framework of the Paris Agreement will build on these arrangements. This section describes these transparency arrangements brieﬂy in order to analyze their scope and workings in Section 4 through the lens of our analytical framework. We organize the overview in four subsections related to transparency arrangements under: (i) the Framework Convention itself, (ii) its Kyoto Protocol, (iii) the Cancún Agreements, and (iv) the Paris Agreement. 3.1. Transparency arrangements under the UNFCCC The UNFCCC calls on all countries to submit “national communications” in which they report on progress with implementation of various climate-related obligations under the Convention. By the end of 2016, all developed country (Annex I) parties had submitted their sixth national communications (which were due in January 2014).2 These national communications by developed countries are subject to regular in-depth reviews by expert review teams (ERTs), comprising of experts nominated by parties and, at times, from intergovernmental organizations (UNFCCC 2014). The ERTs review the data and information provided by countries and assess progress made. The process is intended to be non-political, facilitative, and transparent. The reviews usually include in-country visits in addition to desk-based studies, although centralized reviews are possible for economies in transition with low emission levels. The reports are forwarded to the UNFCCC’s Subsidiary Body for Implementation (SBI), and are Transparency in climate politics A. Gupta and H. van Asselt © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Page 6: made publicly available. National communications are also required from developing countries under the UNFCCC, but these are not subject to review. In addition to national communications, all parties to the UNFCCC need to communicate national greenhouse gas (GHG) inventories. Developed country parties need to prepare inventory reports that follow a common format and guidelines, and adhere to procedural reporting standards of transparency, consistency, comparability, completeness, and accuracy (UNFCCC 2013). Since 2003, these GHG inventory reports have also been subject to a technical review process in which the UNFCCC secretariat carries out initial checks of individual reports and synthesizes the information from all reports, followed by detailed reviews by ERTs (UNFCCC 2014). Such reviews include desk-based and centralized reviews, as well as in-country visits. The review reports are made publicly available. Developing country parties are not required to submit separate inventory reports, but they are required to include the results of their GHG inventories in their national communications. 3.2. Transparency arrangements under the Kyoto Protocol Building on the reporting and review requirements of the UNFCCC noted above, the Kyoto Protocol introduced further transparency arrangements for Annex I parties, requiring them to report annually on (and demonstrate compliance with) their Kyoto emission reduction targets. Given the crucial role of emissions accounting for the environmental integrity of the Kyoto Protocol, the information in these reports is more detailed than that required for national communications under the UNFCCC (UNFCCC 2005a). Further details to be included in such reporting are use of the Kyoto Protocol’s market-based ﬂexibility mechanisms, policies and measures, the provision of ﬁnance, and the transfer of technology. In addition to the regular report, the Kyoto Protocol also requires several “one-off” reports from Annex I countries: (i) an initial report clarifying parties’ “assigned amounts” (i.e. allowed level of emissions) under the Protocol; (ii) a report on the “demonstrable progress” made with the implementation of commitments by 2005; and (iii) a “true-up period” report, through which compliance with Kyoto emission reduction obligations can be assessed. With the exception of demonstrable progress reports, all of these reports are reviewed by ERTs. Thus, ERTs are responsible for a number of speciﬁc reviews (UNFCCC 2005b): • Annual review of GHG inventory reports: This review, which includes the review under the Framework Convention for developed country Kyoto parties, starts with an initial check for completeness, timeliness, consistency, accuracy, transparency, and comparability, followed by an in-depth review that may involve an in-country visit. ERTs can identify problems and, if necessary, apply “adjustments” to the emissions data. • Periodic review of national communications: This review, which also covers the review under the Convention for developed country parties, includes an in-country visit, along with centralized or desk-based reviews. National communications are checked for completeness, and ERTs carry out a detailed examination of their various sections. The report needs to include a technical review and discuss potential problems identiﬁed in the national communication, linked to the criteria of transparency, completeness, and timeliness. • Review of initial reports: This review, which was carried out in 2007–2009, focused on the inventory and systems in place for estimating emissions and keeping track of transfers of emission units to ensure that the appropriate systems were in place at the start of the Protocol’s ﬁrst commitment period in 2008. • Review of “true-up period” reports: This review, carried out in 2015, was conducted to determine whether parties had complied with their Kyoto targets.3 ERTs checked whether the information was provided according to guidelines, whether it was consistent with other information sources, and whether a country met its target. Expert review teams are thus mandated to carry out technical assessments and are also permitted to raise “questions of implementation,” although they are to refrain from political judgments. If these questions cannot be resolved, the ERT can refer the matter to the Kyoto Protocol’s Compliance Committee, which may adopt a variety of measures to promote compliance. In this context, it is useful to brieﬂy note the compliance mechanism established by the Kyoto Protocol, given speciﬁc emission reduction obligations for Annex I parties contained herein. This mechanism consists of a Compliance Committee with a Facilitative Branch and an Enforcement Branch. The compliance procedure of the Kyoto Protocol can be “triggered” in three ways (UNFCCC 2005c): (i) ERTs may, as noted above, raise questions of implementation A. Gupta and H. van Asselt Transparency in climate politics 6 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 7: with regard to the reports submitted; (ii) a party may refer itself to the Compliance Committee; and (iii) another party may refer a party. The mandate of the Enforcement Branch is quite broad, and includes compliance with the emissions- related commitments of Annex I parties, as well as key reporting requirements. If a party is found to be non-compliant, it may need to draft a compliance action plan, be barred from participation in the Protocol’s ﬂexibility mechanisms, or be required to further reduce emissions in the second commitment period of the Protocol (2013–2020). The Facilitative Branch is mandated to address any questions of compliance not addressed by the Enforcement Branch, and is intended to provide an early warning function for non-compliance with emission targets. Its available means are softer than those of the Enforcement Branch, and it is mainly aimed at providing advice and facilitating assistance. Since its commencement in 2006, the Compliance Committee has successfully dealt with several cases of non-compliance, but these have mainly related to methodological issues and reporting rather than to the Kyoto targets themselves (Doelle 2012). 3.3. The transparency arrangements of the Cancún Agreements The 2009 Copenhagen Accord (UNFCCC 2009) not only introduced new, voluntary climate action pledges (for both developed and developing countries) for the period leading up to 2020, but also signaled a new direction for transparency arrangements under the UNFCCC. These arrangements were ﬂeshed out in the Cancún Agreements adopted one year later (UNFCCC 2010). The new transparency arrangements build on the existing system under the UNFCCC (Section 3.1), with more elaborate rules for developed countries. However, they also introduced new reporting and review obligations and processes for developing countries. As per the Cancún Agreements, in addition to already required national communications and annual GHG emissons inventory reports, developed country parties are expected to submit Biennial Reports (BRs) on progress with achieving emission reductions and providing ﬁnance, technology, and capacity-building support to developing countries. The reports follow a speciﬁc format and guidelines (UNFCCC 2011a). BRs are subject to a process of international veriﬁcation, termed “international assessment and review” (IAR), every two years, either independently or together with national communications. International assessment and review includes two separate steps: a technical review and a multilateral assessment. Common guidelines for the technical review of national communications, BRs, and GHG inventories were agreed in 2014 (UNFCCC 2014). When submitted simultaneously, BRs and national communications are subject to a joint in-country review; otherwise, a centralized review is carried out. Technical reviews examine in-depth issues not covered in the inventory review, including information related to emission reduction pledges and the provision of support. The review focuses on transparency, completeness, timeliness, and adherence to reporting guidelines. ERTs can ask questions, request information from the party, and offer advice. The multilateral assessment, the second step in the IAR process, takes place as a state-to-state peer exchange in the UNFCCC context, where the outcomes of the technical review, an Annex I party’s reports, and supplementary information on the achievement of the party’s emission reduction target are considered. Any other parties can submit written questions or raise questions in a session of the SBI devoted to this process. The UNFCCC secretariat drafts a record of the questions and answers, and the SBI forwards conclusions to the Conference of the Parties (COP) to the UNFCCC. The ﬁrst round of multilateral assessments took place at UNFCCC SBI sessions in 2014 and 2015, resulting in a review of 43 developed country parties. The second round started in Marrakesh in November 2016 with a review of 24 parties. As part of the Cancún Agreements, parties also agreed that developing countries – except for least-developed countries (LDCs) and small island developing states (SIDS) – should submit new Biennial Update Reports (BURs) every two years from 2014 onwards, either in conjunction with already required national communications or separately (UNFCCC 2010). BURs should include information on, among others, national circumstances and institutional arrangements, mitigation actions, and ﬁnancial, technical, and capacity needs (UNFCCC 2011a). In a signﬁcant departure from earlier processes, these reports are also to be considered internationally, via a process of “international consultation and analysis” (ICA), in order to “increase transparency of [developing country] mitigation actions and their effects” (UNFCCC 2010, para. 63). The ICA process is expressly intended to be non-punitive and non-intrusive, and to respect national sovereignty. For the purposes of ICA, LDCs and SIDS can be analyzed in groups, rather than individually. The process in an ICA somewhat mirrors the two-step IAR process described above Transparency in climate politics A. Gupta and H. van Asselt © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 7 Page 8: for Annex I countries. It starts with an analysis of a party’s BUR by a team of technical experts in consultation with a party (UNFCCC 2011a). Based on the experts’ report, a multilateral state-to-state “facilitative sharing of views” takes place, which includes questions and answers between parties. The ﬁrst such sessions took place during two SBI workshops in 2016, covering a total of 20 developing country parties (including Brazil, Mexico, South Africa, and South Korea).4 3.4. The Paris Agreement’s enhanced transparency framework With the success of the Paris Agreement hinging upon the effective implementation of the non-legally binding NDCs, transparency plays a central role in the new agreement. The Paris Agreement establishes an “enhanced transparency framework” applicable to all parties (Article 13). The framework provides for “built-in ﬂexibility” in applying a common framework, taking into account parties’ different capacities. Although details of the transparency framework are still under negotiation, the Paris Agreement and its accompanying Decision (UNFCCC 2015) offer some initial insights into how the framework will be designed. First, the new transparency arrangements are to build on the existing ones under the UNFCCC, meaning that elements of the existing reporting and review process will be drawn upon – although which elements will be retained remains uncertain. Moreover, the new system will ultimately supersede existing transparency arrangements (UNFCCC 2015, para. 99). Second, the framework applies to both “action” (i.e. measures to mitigate or adapt to climate change) and “support” (i.e. ﬁnancial, technological, and capacity-building assistance), making the latter a clearer and more prominent focus of UNFCCC transparency arrangements. Speciﬁcally, the Agreement requires developed countries to report biennially on the support provided, and encourages other parties providing support to do so on a voluntary basis. It also envisages, for the ﬁrst time, that developing countries should report on support received. Third, in terms of reporting, the Paris Agreement speciﬁes that each party needs to submit annual GHG inventory reports, as well as “[i]nformation necessary to track progress made in implementing and achieving its [NDC]” (Article 13.7) on a biennial basis (except for LDCs and SIDS; see UNFCCC 2015). Fourth, like the arrangements established by the Cancún Agreements, the international review process will include two main elements: a technical expert review and a process of “facilitative, multilateral consideration of progress.” The technical expert review is to identify “areas of improvement” for the party under review and examine the consistency of the reported information with multilaterally agreed guidelines. The state-to-state multilateral consideration of progress focuses on the implementation and achievement of NDCs, as well as the obligations related to providing climate ﬁnance. 4. Enhancing accountability through transparency: Assessing the prospects In this section, we apply the analytical framework developed in Section 2 to assess whether and how these evolving transparency arrangements serve to further accountability. As such, we analyze, in greater detail, the relations, standards, and judgments that constitute evolving multilateral climate transparency arrangements. 4.1. Relations This crucial element of the accountability problématique goes to the heart of whether and how transparency is able to further accountability in international climate governance. The UNFCCC context is one of state-to-state accountability, so the key question here is who, that is, which states should be accountable. This question lies at the heart of the notion of differentiation in the UNFCCC context. Differential treatment of developed and developing countries has consistently been the make-or-break issue in multilateral climate politics (Rajamani 2013). It has shaped the fate and effectiveness of the UNFCCC regime, whether as a primary reason for United States President George W. Bush to withdraw from participation in the Kyoto Protocol in 2001, or in contributing to the breakdown of the Copenhagen meeting in 2009. Differentiation is a lightning rod for conﬂict because it underpins fundamental disagreements about historical and continuing responsibility and burden sharing for climate actions. These conﬂicts over differentiating between developed and developing countries over who has to do what necessarily spill over into who has to be transparent about what. As we see from the discussion in the previous section, transparency arrangements under both the UNFCCC and the Kyoto Protocol have been consistently differentiated. For instance, while national communications have to be submitted by all countries, the reporting requirements and timing are more ﬂexible for LDCs and SIDS. Moreover, A.
